nativeRange.habitat: Found in fast-moving hill streams and rocky, gravelly pools, where the water is well-oxygenated and flow is moderate to strong.

diet.notes: Primarily carnivorous in the wild, feeding on aquatic molluscs, insects, and worms. In captivity, Yoyo Loaches tend to accept quality sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, Tubifex, brine shrimp, and blanched vegetables. They are well-known consumers of pest snails and can help bring a snail population under control, though they should not be relied upon as the sole means of snail management. Small shrimp are also at risk in a tank that houses them.

imageCandidates.notes: Lukáš Mižoch, 1024x600. Sharp single Yoyo Loach in a planted tank clearly showing the silvery body with dark reticulated Y/O markings; best framed and best focused.
lienyuan lee, 1772x1329. Whole live fish over pebbles showing the bold black Y/O reticulated banding; different angle/individual for variety.
Orishas92, 2816x2112. High-res live specimen foraging on gravel; markings visible but image is slightly soft, so kept as a non-recommended extra.

summary: The Yoyo Loach (Botia almorhae) is an active, social freshwater fish from the hill streams of northern India and Nepal, recognized by the alternating Y and O markings along its sides. It tends to form tight social hierarchies and will consume pest snails. Best kept in groups of five or more in a 200-litre tank.
careNotes:
The common name 'Yoyo Loach' refers to the alternating 'Y' and 'O' markings along the body, which can become less distinct with age (a process called anastomosis). 'Pakistani Loach' is a common but inaccurate trade name — confirmed distribution is the Ganges river drainage in northern India and Nepal; wild specimens from Pakistan have not been consistently documented.

Yoyo Loaches form complex social hierarchies within the group. The alpha (typically the largest female) sets the group's temperament. When kept singly or in pairs, individuals tend to become withdrawn or show increased aggression. Groups of 5 or more are generally recommended, with 10+ considered ideal. Dominance interactions include 'greying out' (temporary loss of patterning), shadowing behavior, and audible clicking sounds produced by pharyngeal teeth or subocular spine movement.

Yoyo Loaches are considered more susceptible to ich than many other aquarium fish, particularly when water temperatures fall toward the lower end of their range — keeping the tank at the warmer end (around 26–28°C) is generally advised when disease pressure is suspected. Like Clown Loaches, they have relatively thin scales (not fully scaleless) and tend to be more sensitive to copper-based medications and standard ich treatments; half doses or copper-free alternatives are commonly recommended. 'Skinny disease' caused by Spironucleus flagellates has been reported in newly imported specimens — treatment with levamisole or fenbendazole has been documented.

They possess sharp, motile subocular spines that can erect when the fish is stressed; care is advised when netting to avoid entanglement. Tank lids are recommended as these fish have been known to jump.

Cross-reference: [clown-loach], [dwarf-chain-loach], [zebra-loach] — all family Botiidae, similar care requirements.
breedingNotes: No confirmed breeding reports exist for Yoyo Loaches in private aquaria. In the wild, spawning appears to be linked to seasonal upstream migration into hillstream environments. Commercial producers are known to use hormone injection to induce spawning, and hybrids have appeared in the trade. Breeding is generally considered not feasible for hobbyist setups.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 15.5 cm SL, family Botiidae, order Cypriniformes, range India and Nepal.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (75-86°F / 24-30°C), pH 6.5-7.5, gH 3-10, min tank 40 gal (151 L), lifespan 5-8 years, careLevel intermediate. Temperature range averaged with SeriouslyFish (19-27.5°C) to produce 22-28°C. Tank size biased toward SeriouslyFish's larger 120x45cm recommendation (~243 L), rounded to 200 L for a school.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op (loach overview)
additional.notes: No dedicated yoyo loach page found. Overview confirms adult size 13-15 cm, group of 6+ recommended, semi-aggressive/ornery temperament, avoid invertebrates.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed breeding reports exist for Yoyo Loaches in private aquaria. Commercial producers use hormone injection to induce spawning, and hybrids have appeared in the trade.
